# Orca v2 API

Orca is a build orchestrator that manages a graph of actions (agent tasks
and commands) connected by conditional edges. Actions execute serially via
a scheduler. The graph can be read and modified at runtime through this
REST API.

## Base URL
http://localhost:7072

## Authentication
None required.

## Quick Reference

### Actions
POST   /actions                Create an action
                               Body: { id, type, project_id, status?, params?, tags? }
                               type: "agent" | "command"
                               Returns 201. Returns 409 if id exists.

GET    /actions                List actions
                               Query: ?tag=, ?status=, ?type=

GET    /actions/:id            Get action detail (includes edges and history)

PATCH  /actions/:id            Update action fields
                               Body: { params?, tags?, status? }

DELETE /actions/:id            Delete action (cascades edges and history)

POST   /actions/:id/retry      Reset action to pending (increments iteration)
POST   /actions/:id/skip       Mark action as skipped
POST   /actions/:id/respond    Complete a waiting action
                               Body: { status, summary, notes? }

### Groups (template expansion)
POST   /groups                 Create a full task chain from a template
                               Body: { id, template, project_id,
                                       prompt?, after?, depends_on?,
                                       overrides?, tags?, budget? }
                               Reads templates from project.orca.yaml on disk.
                               Creates all actions + intra-task edges.
                               Wires pass edge from "after" to first action.
                               Returns 201 with { actions: [], edges: number }.

### Edges
POST   /edges                  Create an edge
                               Body: { from_action, to_action, condition }
                               condition: pass|fail|error|stuck|timeout|
                                          cost_exceeded|max_turns

DELETE /edges/:id              Delete an edge

### Import
POST   /import                 Import a full YAML config
                               PREFERRED: { dir: "/absolute/path/to/project" }
                                 Reads project.orca.yaml from the directory.
                                 Resolves project_dir relative to that directory.
                                 Custom spec: { dir: "...", spec_path: "custom.yaml" }
                               ALSO ACCEPTED: { yaml: "...", source_dir: "/abs/path" }
                                 source_dir is REQUIRED — it resolves relative paths
                                 in the YAML (like project_dir: ".").
                               Creates project, actions, and edges.

POST   /reimport               Re-import specific tasks from a config
                               Body: { dir: "path", tasks: ["task-id", ...] }
                               Replaces specified tasks, preserves others.

IMPORTANT: Always use { dir: "/absolute/path" } for imports. This is the
safest method — it reads the YAML from disk and automatically resolves
all relative paths correctly. Do NOT inline YAML content unless you also
provide source_dir. Relative project_dir without source_dir will be rejected.

### Executor
POST   /executor/pause         Pause the scheduler
POST   /executor/resume        Resume the scheduler
GET    /executor/status        Get scheduler state, active action, counts

### Events (SSE)
GET    /events                 Global SSE stream
                               Events: connected, action_started,
                               action_completed, action_waiting,
                               edge_traversed, executor_state, stats,
                               tool_use, unhandled_failure

GET    /actions/:id/events     Per-action SSE stream (filtered)
GET    /actions/:id/logs       Get JSONL tool call log entries for an action

### System
GET    /health                 Health check with stats
GET    /                       Discovery document (Accept: application/json)
                               or HTML dashboard link

## Key Concepts

**Action**: A unit of work. Type is "agent" (LLM) or "command" (shell).
Has status (inactive/pending/running/completed/failed/waiting/skipped),
params, output, iteration count, cost. Agent actions need a prompt in
params. Command actions need a command in params.

**Edge**: A conditional connection. When an action completes with a
condition (pass/fail/error/etc), the executor follows matching edges
to activate the next action. An action can have edges to different
targets for different conditions.

**Group**: A task chain created from a template via POST /groups.
One API call creates multiple actions with proper edges. Example:
a "tdd" template creates write-tests → develop → eval with
fail-back edges from eval to develop.

**Template**: Defined in project.orca.yaml. Specifies action types,
edges, and default params for a reusable task pattern. Templates
are read from disk when POST /groups is called.

**Supervisor**: An action tagged "type:supervisor". When any action
fails with no matching outgoing edge, the executor automatically
activates the supervisor with failure context in its params
(failed_action, failed_condition, failed_output).

**Project**: A record with id, project_dir, model, scope. Created
by POST /import. Required for project_id on actions. The project_dir
determines the working directory for all actions AND which nix shell
environment is used (auto-detected from flake.nix/shell.nix in that dir).

## Edge Conditions

- pass: action completed successfully (status: "passed")
- fail: action explicitly failed (status: "failed")
- error: malformed/unexpected output (status: "unknown" or missing)
- stuck: same output repeated 3 times
- timeout: action timed out
- cost_exceeded: budget exceeded
- max_turns: agent hit max turn limit

Default edges (from config expansion): pass → next action, all
failure conditions → first action in task. Self-loops are skipped.

## How to Import a Pipeline

The recommended way to import a pipeline is to point to the project
directory on disk. The project should have a project.orca.yaml file:

```bash
# RECOMMENDED: point to the project directory
curl -X POST http://localhost:7072/import \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"dir": "/home/user/projects/my-project"}'
```

This reads project.orca.yaml from the directory, resolves all paths
relative to that directory (project_dir, nix detection, working
directory for commands and agents), and creates the full action graph.

Do NOT read the YAML file yourself and pass its contents inline.
The dir-based import handles path resolution automatically and avoids
a class of bugs where relative paths resolve against the wrong base.

If you must pass YAML content directly (e.g., generating config on
the fly), you MUST include source_dir:

```bash
# ONLY if generating YAML dynamically — include source_dir
curl -X POST http://localhost:7072/import \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"yaml": "name: my-proj\ntasks: ...", "source_dir": "/home/user/projects/my-project"}'
```

## Writing Good Prompts

The most important part of any pipeline is the prompts. Each agent
stage should get its own tailored prompt via overrides — do NOT use
a single shared prompt for write-tests, develop, and qa.

Key rules:
- **write-tests**: Specify test file path, exact test cases, imports.
  Say "Do NOT implement source code."
- **develop**: Name specific files to create/modify. Say "Read src/
  first for conventions. Only implement what the tests require."
- **eval**: Scope to this task's tests (`bun test test/auth.test.ts`),
  not all tests (`bun test`).
- **qa**: Specify how to test (curl for APIs, browser MCP tools for
  frontends). Include server start/stop instructions.
- **Scope strictly**: Do NOT tell agents to read roadmap files or
  describe features beyond the current task. Agents will try to
  build anything you mention.

See /docs/guides/config-format.md for full prompt-writing guidelines
with examples.

## Common Patterns

Import a project:
  curl -X POST /import -d '{"dir":"/home/user/projects/my-project"}'

Create a task chain with per-stage prompts:
  curl -X POST /groups -d '{
    "id":"auth","template":"tdd","project_id":"my-proj",
    "overrides":{
      "write-tests":{"prompt":"Write auth tests in test/auth.test.ts..."},
      "develop":{"prompt":"Implement auth in src/auth.ts..."},
      "eval":{"command":"bun test test/auth.test.ts"}
    }
  }'

Wire tasks in sequence:
  curl -X POST /groups -d '{"id":"api","template":"tdd","project_id":"p","prompt":"Build API","after":"auth.commit"}'

Check graph state:
  curl /actions | jq '[.[] | {id, status}]'

Activate an action:
  curl -X PATCH /actions/my.action -d '{"status":"pending"}'

Retry a failed action:
  curl -X POST /actions/my.action/retry

## Guides
For detailed documentation on specific topics:
- Config format & prompt writing: /docs/guides/config-format.md
- Templates & per-stage prompts: /docs/guides/templates.md
- Dynamic tasking: /docs/guides/dynamic-tasking.md
- Supervisor & recovery: /docs/guides/supervisor.md

## Full OpenAPI Spec
/docs/openapi.yaml
